issue_title: Missing DETAIL_KEYS for "additional_purpose" in transaction details
issue_body: In `mt940.processors._parse_mt940_details`, values for detail keys 20 thru 29 are concatenated since they together constitute the `purpose` field. The [linked spec](https://www.db-bankline.deutsche-bank.com/download/MT940_Deutschland_Structure2002.pdf) says keys 60 thru 63 should likewise be treated as the `additional_purpose` field, however only key 60 is considered. Is that on purpose (no pun intended ;-) , or may I fix it?

issue_title: The pew shell config breaks my shell
issue_body:
I'm not sure where the problem comes from. It could be pew, it could be bash, maybe even vte, who knows?

All I know is that when I add these lines to my `~/.bashrc`, then my shell starts misbehaving:

```bash
if [ -f /usr/bin/pew ]; then
  source $(pew shell_config)
fi
```

The symptoms are that when using the arrow keys to navigate through older commands, eventually the beginning of the command line gets messed up.

Here is an example session:

* the lines above are in my `~/.bashrc`
* I start a new shell (and so it has sourced the `~/.bashrc`
* I press the `up` arrow, the command line shows the last command I entered, i.e `sudo dnf repoquery --whatprovides 'libwebkit2gtk-4.0.so.37()(64bit)'`
* I press the `up` arrow again, the command line now shows `sudorpm -q --requires epiphany`
    The command I really had entered was `rpm -q --requires epiphany`
* now I press and hold the `backspace` key, to erase the command line, and it clears everything, until the `sudo` word, which can never be cleaned (at that point if I use the `up` or `down` arrows to navigate the history again, that `sudo` word will always be there, prepended to the command displayed)
* if I now enter a command, say `ls` for example, then what I see is `sudols`, but what gets executed is just `ls`

So the display is completely broken, with the first "word" of a command remaining stuck at the beginning of a line.

It is extremely disturbing, as it breaks the mental flow of typing commands making you wonder why this word is there and how to remove it.

Another symptom is that long lines get wrapped badly:

![screenshot from 2016-07-11 11-04-54](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/272170/16725405/66a09ea6-4757-11e6-92d2-9c1a71f74d6e.png)

Instead, I would expect:

![screenshot from 2016-07-11 11-05-18](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/272170/16725414/6ff2678c-4757-11e6-8c9b-e50770b27f9e.png)

And I properly get the latter when I **don't** source the pew shell config.

As it is, it pretty much destroys my shell experience, and so I've reverted to not sourcing the pew shell config.

issue_title: Adding new tasks to a `curio.wait`
issue_body:
I have a set of tasks that are tracked(?) by a `curio.wait` object to check if they are finished.  If the tasks crash, or if I need to add a new task to this list, I have no way of rebooting them and re-inserting them into the wait without cancelling all tasks and re-creating the wait, or using the internal private API.

It'd be nice if there was a way to insert a new task into a waiter without having to re-create it.

issue_title: Equality is not transitive
issue_body:
```python
>>> from pint import UnitRegistry
>>> u1 = UnitRegistry()
>>> u2 = UnitRegistry()
>>> v1 = 1*u1.dimensionless
>>> v2 = 1*u2.dimensionless
>>> v1 == 1
True
>>> v2 == 1
True
>>> v1 == v2
ValueError: Cannot operate with Unit and Unit of different registries.
```
Is there a justification for not allowing _dimensionless_ quantities to be compared between unit registries?

issue_title: About strand information in spliced alignment
issue_body:
Hi Heng,

I just used minimap2 (version 2.2) for spliced alignment of PacBio reads to human genome.
I used “-ax splice” option.

For some reason, the minimap2 output alignments do not contain strand information. We expected that spliced reads would contain the XS tag, but my minimap2 output does not contain any XS tag. 

I am guessing that “-u b” option may be able to provide the XS tag for the spliced read. But the “-x splice” option already includes the -ub option according to the minimap2 2.2 manual. So maybe I was missing something else here?

I’d greatly appreciate your advice.

Thank you very much!

issue_body: Interface blocks for a over minute on a twitter dataset containing 200 twitter events, this is too slow. PARSEME reports similar delays. Find and fix bottleneck.

issue_title: Combiner.preview() fails
issue_body: `Combiner` inherits `preview` from `Transformer` but it needs to be overwritten because the base call is different for multiple inputs.

issue_title: Plugin reload
issue_body: When writing new plugins, the ability to reload would be nice.  Further, my experience with other bots has shown that it's really helpful to the non-programmer to be able to reload a crashed plugin.

issue_title: Project missing tags for recent releases
issue_body: I usually check the [releases](https://github.com/skoczen/will/releases) page to see what the current version of will is, but it's still stuck on 0.4.10. Bringing that up to date will make it easier for people to know what the current release is and see what changed between versions.

issue_title: Introspective Unit Test
issue_body:
As a poor Fortraner I was always fascinated by the introspective-power of Python. One of the amazing Python module that I learned to love is [doctest](https://docs.python.org/2/library/doctest.html). In a few words

> The doctest module searches for pieces of text that look like interactive Python sessions, and then executes those sessions to verify that they work exactly as shown.

For example, in a code like the following
```python
def fib(n):
    """ 
    Calculates the n-th Fibonacci number iteratively 
    >>> fib(0)
    0
    >>> fib(1)
    1
    >>> fib(10) 
    55
    """
    return 0
``` 
the **comments** like `>>> fib(0)` are *VALID UNIT TESTS*! When you run the above code with `doctest` module all the *test-comments* are tested and their results compared with the expected results (provided immediately after each test in the above example).

During the last night (when Angelica crying...), I figured out that such a behavior can be *mimic* by FoBiS.py for Fortran codes (with some limitations).   

### API desiderata
Let us consider the following Fortran module.
```fortran
module foo
use bar, only : make_bar
implicit none
private
public :: type_foo, make_foo
type :: type_foo
    !FBStest
    !>>> type(type_foo) :: my_foo
    !>>> call my_foo%init(b=992)
    !>>> call my_foo%print()
    !> 992
    !FBSendtest
    private
    integer :: a
    contains
      procedure, pass(self) :: init
      procedure, pass(self) :: print
  endtype type_foo
  contains
    ! type bound procedures
    subroutine init(self, b)
    class(type_foo), intent(INOUT) :: self
    integer,         intent(IN) ::    b
    self%a = b
    endsubroutine init

    subroutine print(self)
    class(type_foo), intent(IN) :: self
    print*, self%a
    endsubroutine print

    ! non type bound procedures
    function make_foo(b) result(foo_object)
    !FBStest
    !>>> type(type_foo) :: my_foo
    !>>> my_foo = make_foo(b=102)
    !>>> call my_foo%print()
    !> 102
    !FBSendtest
    integer, intent(IN) :: b
    type(type_foo) ::      foo_object
    foo_object%a = b
    endfunction make_foo
endmodule foo
```

FoBiS.py can:
+ build the module with all its dependencies;
+ recognize the FoBiS.py doctest, namely the blocks `FBStest`/`FBSendtest`;
+ for each doctest:
    + create, on the fly, a small *volatile program test* for each doctest, **using** the module containing the doctest itself;
    + run the doctest:
    + capture the result printed on stdout and compare with the expected one;
+ print a summary of each doctest executed.

### volatile program test skeleton

Fortran is less flexible than Python, than we must use a more verbose syntax for the doctest. In fact, in the above example, the fortran doctest has also the definition of the variable `my_foo` that is not necessary in the Python doctest. From this point of view the definition of the *volatile program test* corresponding to each doctest is crucial. In general, a possible skeleton could be:

```fortran
program volatile_doctest
use foo
implicit none
! user-defined doctest here
endprogram volatile_doctest 
```

The doctests of the above example are

##### type bound test
```fortran
program volatile_doctest
use foo
implicit none
type(type_foo) :: my_foo
call my_foo%init(b=992)
call my_foo%print()
endprogram volatile_doctest 
``` 
##### non type bound test
```fortran
program volatile_doctest
use foo
implicit none
type(type_foo) :: my_foo
my_foo = make_foo(b=102)
call my_foo%print()
endprogram volatile_doctest 
``` 

### Limitations

The approach above described should be very easy to implement into the current FoBiS.py version, but the main limitations are:
+ the procedures that can be tested are only those defined inside a module;
+ we must pass through the standard output for checking the results.

### Considerations

Your thinking, why? The reasons are:
+ why not?;
+ because I think it is very useful an atomic/micro testing-framework for testing each procedures;
+ because it is very nice/elegant/clean/maintainable to embed the test into the procedure docstring;
+ because Fortran is better than Python, thus It must have its doctest module :-)

There many limitations (some above pointed-out), nevertheless such a feature is very useful for my OCD programming approach and I will try to implement it soon.

What do you think about?

issue_title: Feature request: better/more verbose output
issue_body: It would be nice if there was a flag to increase the verbosity of FoBiS.py to help trouble shoot problems. There should be a flag that makes FoBiS.py output what commands it is running as it runs them. Right now, when something goes wrong it is too opaque as to whether the problem was in FoBiS.py (a bug) or if the user did something wrong. For example, see https://github.com/jacobwilliams/json-fortran/issues/53 where the problem was an extra space was introduced into the build script. If there had been some sort of step by step debugging output it would have been easier to see that flags weren’t passed into FoBiS.py due to an error in the shell script.

issue_title: Add possibility to have 2 repositories instead of 1 in diff/diff_summary functions
issue_body:
`svn diff` normally allows to have 2 urls or targets: an old one and a new one.

```
diff (di): Display local changes or differences between two revisions or paths.
usage: 1. diff
       2. diff [-c M | -r N[:M]] [TARGET[@REV]...]
       3. diff [-r N[:M]] --old=OLD-TGT[@OLDREV] [--new=NEW-TGT[@NEWREV]] \
               [PATH...]
       4. diff OLD-URL[@OLDREV] NEW-URL[@NEWREV]
       5. diff OLD-URL[@OLDREV] NEW-PATH[@NEWREV]
       6. diff OLD-PATH[@OLDREV] NEW-URL[@NEWREV]
```

It would be nice to have an optional arguments in functions `diff` and `diff_summary` to have a new target.

issue_title: Using daff with webpack
issue_body:
Any reports of successfully using daff with webpack? I was trying to include daff in a React+Relay project that uses webpack and so far, it has proved to be fairly painful. 

I've created a minimal example repo (just webpack and daff, no React) at https://github.com/eddies/daff-webpack. 

Just to generate the bundle (i.e. run webpack), I needed to `npm install daff --no-optional` and add

```
externals: [
    'child_process',
    'fibers',
    'fs',
    'sqlite3'
  ],
```
to `webpack.config.js`, otherwise I get a whole mess of warnings and errors related to the above modules.

However, even with the bundle built, I'm faced with a bunch of further errors at runtime just importing daff, mostly (I believe) because of all the tests in daff.js that use `typeof exports != "undefined"` to determine whether it's running in Node or a browser.

I made some manual edits to daff.js, substituting `isNode` for `typeof exports != "undefined"`, where `isNode` is given by:

```
var isNode = Object.prototype.toString.call(typeof process !== 'undefined' ? process : 0) === '[object process]';
```

(That's what [detect-node](https://github.com/iliakan/detect-node/blob/master/index.js) uses). That at least let me `import daff from 'daff';`. But actually using daff (e.g. using TableView in the example repo linked above) still blows up quite spectacularly. It would please me to no end to find out I'm just doing something wrong here, but I'm not sure what to try next.

issue_title: Rednose and processes do not work togeather
issue_body:
Hi, I've run into a bizarre issue with rednose, python 3.5.2, Ubuntu 16.04.
Problem: when multiple inheritance is used rednose discards the failure. It depends on the order of defining test classes/methods.

The code to reproduce the problem:
$ cat test_rednose.py 
```
import unittest


class FrontendCase(unittest.TestCase):
    pass

class BackendCase(unittest.TestCase):
    pass

class BackendUserCase(BackendCase):
    pass

class ExistingBackendUserCase(BackendUserCase):
    pass

class FrontendUserCase(FrontendCase, BackendUserCase):
    pass

class ExistingFrontendUserCase(FrontendUserCase, ExistingBackendUserCase):
    pass

class Test_0(FrontendUserCase):
    def test_dummy(self):
        pass


class Test_1(ExistingFrontendUserCase):
    def test_assertion(self):
        print("Should fail")
        self.assertTrue(False)
```

$ cat nosetests.cfg 
```
[nosetests]
verbosity = 1
with-doctest = 0
logging-level = DEBUG
logging-format = "%(asctime)s %(name)-12s %(levelname)-8s %(module)s:%(lineno)s %(message)s"
nocapture = 1
nologcapture = 1
rednose = 1
processes = 1
```

Run it:
```

$ nosetests -c nosetests.cfg test_rednose.py 
Should fail
.
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 0.031s

OK
```

Now, comment out rednose in nosetests.cfg and run again:
```
$ grep -v rednose nosetests.cfg > nosetests.without_rednose.cfg
$ nosetests -c nosetests.without_rednose.cfg test_rednose.py 
.F
======================================================================
FAIL: test_assertion (test_rednose.Test_1)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "./test_rednose.py", line 30, in test_assertion
    self.assertTrue(False)
AssertionError: False is not true

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 2 tests in 0.020s

FAILED (failures=1)
```

Voila, finally assertTrue(False) fails as it should.

This issue doesn't seem to be related to "--processes" in nosetests.
What is really bizarre is that:
a) if we just remove Test_0::test_dummy method (leaving Test_0 class empty), the assertion will properly fail.
b) The test method in Test_1 class is actually executed up to that assertion point.

I didn't debug rednose itself.


$ python -V
Python 3.5.2
$ uname -rmpov
4.4.0-62-generic #83-Ubuntu SMP Wed Jan 18 14:10:15 UTC 2017 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux

issue_title: Support providing local (client) certificate file including private key
issue_body:
Python's SSL module supports presenting a client certificate via `load_cert_chain()`. Relevant docs:

https://docs.python.org/3/library/ssl.html#ssl.SSLContext.load_cert_chain

Note how `keyfile` is not _required_.

About that: https://docs.python.org/3/library/ssl.html#combined-key-and-certificate

Quote:
```
Often the private key is stored in the same file as the certificate; in this case,
only the certfile parameter to SSLContext.load_cert_chain() and
wrap_socket() needs to be passed. If the private key is stored with the
certificate, it should come before the first certificate in the certificate chain:

-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----
... (private key in base64 encoding) ...
-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
... (certificate in base64 PEM encoding) ...
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
```

ldap3 on the one hand uses `load_cert_chain()` and exposes the two main arguments to the user of ldap3, but it requires both of them (only invokes `load_cert_chain()` if a key file was provided):

https://github.com/cannatag/ldap3/blob/b5abdee3fe38c56950a782cd8d29e59631aae967/ldap3/core/tls.py#L177

It's quite convenient to have key and cert live in the same file. We should change that condition and invoke `ssl_context.load_cert_chain()` even if only `local_certificate_file` was provided.

issue_title: Images from doctests should be opt-in, not opt-out
issue_body:
[Currently][1] it's necessary to set `NO_IMAGES=True` (or any value) for running doctests. This should be the opposite (i.e. `nox -s docs_images` should have to set an environment variable).

(It's also worth looking into `pytest` support for doctests.)

[1]: https://github.com/dhermes/bezier/blob/eaec7cfbba84cfb27247647be52286551df9e619/nox.py#L199

issue_title: Consider adding "compensated" evaluation via error-free transformations
issue_body:
There are three applications I have in mind:

- Evaluating `B(s)` for a Bézier curves and producing `Bhat, err` where `Bhat` is the value computed and `err` is a running error.
  - See [Accurate evaluation of a polynomial and its derivative in Bernstein form][3] (EFT for de Casteljau algorithm)
- Evaluating `B1(s) - B2(t)` for two Bézier curves and producing `Dhat, err`. It **may** be more beneficial to produce the tensor product surface implied by this difference, i.e. if `B1(s) = SUM_i pi B{i,m}(s)` and `B2(t) = SUM_j qj B{j,n}(t)` then the tensor product surface will have control net `pi - qj`.
  - See [Accurate evaluation of Bézier curves and (tensor product) surfaces and the Bernstein-Fourier algorithm][1] (Conclusion: use de Casteljau)
- Evaluating `B(s, t)` for a Bézier surface (or "triangle" or "bivariate") and producing `Bhat, err`.
  - See [Accurate evaluation algorithm for bivariate polynomial in Bernstein-Bézier form (i.e. a "Bézier triangle")][2]

I have started some [investigation][4] of this.

[1]: https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.amc.2015.08.086
[2]: https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apnum.2011.07.009
[3]: https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.camwa.2010.05.021
[4]: https://gist.github.com/dhermes/8999485869531dad1345eacf8acc8c91

issue_title: pyfs keyring fails horribly on pyfs 0.5.
issue_body:
pyfs has released a new, backward-incompatible version that adds Python 3 support. Unfortunately, this new version has serious problems with keyring, probably rooted in fundamental problems in configparser.

Because configparser writes native 'str' objects to the stream (e.g. `fp.write("\n")`), those calls fail on Python 2 if the file is opened in text mode and fail on Python 3 if the file was opened in binary mode (because fs is strict about the input type even on Python 2).

I won't be able to solve this issue, so I'm going to mark the tests as xfail and hope a volunteer can help resolve the issues. If they're not resolved, I'll consider pulling support for pyfs.

issue_title: Lock disappearing?
issue_body:
Hello,

When I run two instances of celerybeat with redbeat scheduler, at first things work as expected -- one instance acquires the lock, and the other waits. After some random time though, the other instance starts working, even though the original instance thinks it still have the lock.

I've made following changes to debug this further:
https://github.com/mbarszcz/redbeat/commit/23475450e8f52ac84ab5c10d813c6583e07d09ff

This is the output I'm getting on Worker A when things go well:

`[2018-02-14 23:58:19,819: DEBUG/MainProcess] Setting default socket timeout to 30

ntent.zip_test.zip_test() content.zip_test.zip_test() <freq: 1.00 minute>
[2018-02-14 23:58:19,827: DEBUG/MainProcess] beat: Ticking with max interval->5.00 se
conds

[2018-02-14 23:58:24,832: DEBUG/MainProcess] beat: Extending lock by 60 seconds...
[2018-02-14 23:58:24,833: DEBUG/MainProcess] Selecting tasks
[...]
`

And output on Worker B looks like this:
`
[2018-02-15 00:00:20,724: DEBUG/MainProcess] Setting default socket timeout to 30
[2018-02-15 00:00:20,725: INFO/MainProcess] beat: Starting...
[2018-02-15 00:00:20,735: DEBUG/MainProcess] Stored entry: <RedBeatSchedulerEntry: content.zip_test.zip_test() content.zip_test.zip_test() <freq: 1.00 minute>

`

After some time though, Worker A reports:

And keeps running. Worker B also starts, because the lock is no longer there:

I can see how I could work around it in redbeat (try to acquire lock again after it disappears), but what might be causing the disappearing lock in the first place? Worker A prolonged it on 00:23:20 by 60 seconds, and 5 seconds later it's no longer there.

issue_title: Offset method robustness for complex shapes
issue_body:
The offset method for shapes that "close" upon themselves (i.e. enlarged such that the resulting polygon structure has overlapping portions) results in  segmentation fault (SIGSEGV). 

As a simple example -- letter C, drawn as a single polygon, and then enlarged using the offset method:

```
import gdspy
import numpy

cell = gdspy.Cell('CELL')
c_coords = numpy.array([  [ 0.6663    ,  0.32629112],
                          [ 0.6663    ,  0.1669    ],
                          [ 0.4994    ,  0.        ],
                          [ 0.1669    ,  0.        ],
                          [ 0.        ,  0.1669    ],
                          [ 0.        ,  0.8331    ],
                          [ 0.1669    ,  1.        ],
                          [ 0.4994    ,  1.        ],
                          [ 0.6663    ,  0.8331    ],
                          [ 0.6663    ,  0.67370888],
                          [ 0.4994    ,  0.67370888],
                          [ 0.4994    ,  0.76396776],
                          [ 0.43026776,  0.8331    ],
                          [ 0.23603224,  0.8331    ],
                          [ 0.1669    ,  0.76396776],
                          [ 0.1669    ,  0.23603224],
                          [ 0.23603224,  0.1669    ],
                          [ 0.43026776,  0.1669    ],
                          [ 0.4994    ,  0.23603224],
                          [ 0.4994    ,  0.32629112]])
poly = gdspy.Polygon(c_coords)
cell.add(poly)
offset_result = gdspy.offset(gdspy.CellReference(cell), distance=0.1)
offset_cell = gdspy.Cell('CELL_OFFSET')
offset_cell.add(offset_result)

gdspy.LayoutViewer(cells=[offset_cell])
```

For distance=0.1 (as above), the offset method returns the expected result. The moment the C-shape "closes" upon itself (distance >= 0.18), the call returns SIGSEGV.

issue_title: Use factory-methods to create instances
issue_body:
Several classes use a Deferred attribute ``.post_bootstrap`` or similar so that calling code knows "when they're ready". This is pretty hacky and not very nice. JP Calderone has a good blog-post about this: http://as.ynchrono.us/2014/12/asynchronous-object-initialization.html

Instead:
 - a Deferred should be passed in to the constructor
 - factory methods (that return Deferreds/@inlineCallbacks) are provided
 - "normal" code calls the factory method

So, for example, instead of creating a ``TorConfig`` and waiting for ``.post_bootstrap`` code like this is preferred:

```python
class TorConfig(object):
    @classmethod
    @inlineCallbacks
    def from_connection(cls, reactor, connection):
        bootstrapped = Deferred()
        cfg = TorConfig(..., bootstrapped)
        yield bootstrapped
        returnValue(cfg)
```

...so now "user" code would do this:

```python
def main(reactor):
    con = yield TorControlProtocol.from_endpoint(reactor, TCP4ClientEndpoint("localhost", 9051))
    cfg = yield TorConfig.from_connection(reactor, con)
task.react(main)
````

issue_title: Strand specific merge does not keep strand information
issue_body:
Hi,

I came across this problem when using merge() function while keeping strandedness for a BedTool object. On the command line it can be done like this and works fine:
```
bedtools merge -s -c 6 -o distinct -i genes.bed 
```
But running the equivalent command in python does not keep the strand information in the pybed object:

```python
merged_genes = genes.merge(s = True, c = 6, o = 'distinct')
merged_genes[0].strand
> '.'
```

I believe this is because the strand will be on the third column of bedtools output and additional columns specified by -c are added afterwards. I found a quick and dirty way to solve it by adding another column before but it would be helpful if the output of merge is corrected .

```python
merged_genes = genes.merge(s = True, c = '5,6', o = 'distinct')
merged_genes[0].strand
> '+'
```
Thanks a lot!
Salma

def handle_autocomplete(fn, row, col):

issue_title: Commit does not update the IPDB cache
issue_body:
Based on the http://docs.pyroute2.org/ipdb.html we note that "So IPDB is updated asynchronously, but the commit() operation is synchronous."
As expected,
```python3 
import pyroute2
IFACE = pyroute2.IPDB()
IFACE.interfaces.eth1.up()
```
does not do anything according to ifconfig eth1 and later running

`IFACE.commit()` causes eth1 to come up.

However, the following causes assertion errors:
```python3
for i in range(100):                                                                                                       
    print(i)
    IFACE.interfaces.eth1.up()
    IFACE.commit()
    #1
    assert IFACE.interfaces.eth1.operstate == 'UP'
    IFACE.interfaces.eth1.down()
    IFACE.commit()
    #2
    assert IFACE.interfaces.eth1.operstate == 'DOWN'
```
If we add IFACE.interfaces.eth1.reload() at 1 and 2, the failures continue. It we add IFACE.initdb() at 1 and 2, the failures continue. If we try to rebuild the IFACE object with IFACE = pyroute2.IPDB() at 1 and 2 the failures continue. However, if we replace the asserts with 
`assert 'UP' in subprocess.check_output('ifconfig eth1')` and `assert 'UP' not in subprocess.check_output('ifconfig eth1')` the failures disappear. This implies that the changes are successfully sent to the kernel, but not being propagated back to the library's IPDB cache. This is corroborated by pausing using pdb and checking the output of `ifconfig eth1` and `IFACE.interfaces.eth1.operstate` until the later updated.

Is there an intended workaround? We have been able to get away with using subprocess, but this seems like an unintended behavior.

issue_title: Indefinite length encoding supported on input?
issue_body:
v0.21.1

Encountered a p12 file using indefinite length encoding in auth_safe. The Content-Info OctetString is not aggregated correctly from the substrings. The reconstructed OctetString is too large and the native value contains the ASN.1 headers of the substrings instead of only content.

Test case

Input string in hex = '2480040d8dfff0980736af936e423acfcc04159277f7f0e479ffc7dc33b2d03d7b1a186d4472aa490000'

[dummy.zip](https://github.com/wbond/asn1crypto/files/812869/dummy.zip)

$ openssl asn1parse -inform DER -in dummy.da2
    0:d=0  hl=2 l=inf  cons: OCTET STRING      
    2:d=1  hl=2 l=  13 prim: OCTET STRING      [HEX DUMP]:8DFFF0980736AF936E423ACFCC
   17:d=1  hl=2 l=  21 prim: OCTET STRING      [HEX DUMP]:9277F7F0E479FFC7DC33B2D03D7B1A186D4472AA49
   40:d=1  hl=2 l=   0 prim: EOC               

Expected behaviour: an OctetString whose native value is a byte string of length 34 
```python
>>> data = binascii.unhexlify('2480040d8dfff0980736af936e423acfcc04159277f7f0e479ffc7dc33b2d03d7b1a186d4472aa490000')
>>> x = core.OctetString.load(data)
>>> len(x.native)
34
>>> binascii.hexlify(x.native)
b'8dfff0980736af936e423acfcc9277f7f0e479ffc7dc33b2d03d7b1a186d4472aa49'
```

Observed behaviour:
```python
>>> data = binascii.unhexlify('2480040d8dfff0980736af936e423acfcc04159277f7f0e479ffc7dc33b2d03d7b1a186d4472aa490000')
>>> x = core.OctetString.load(data)
>>> len(x.native)
38
>>> binascii.hexlify(x.native)
b'040d8dfff0980736af936e423acfcc04159277f7f0e479ffc7dc33b2d03d7b1a186d4472aa49'

## note ASN.1 headers 040d and 0415 are in the native value
```

issue_title: Convert suite to pytest
issue_body:
Hi,

In April the pytest team is holding "adopt pytest month" to encourage projects to consider using pytest, by pairing open source projects with experienced pytest helpers (myself included). The idea is to create some PRs in a fork to port some of your xUnit test suite (which seems pretty complete btw), while discussing and explaining some of pytest's features, like automatic test discovery, plain asserts (no need to use `self.assertX` methods), parametrized tests, plugins, etc.

Since pytest can run standard xUnit tests, you can convert tests to use the full power of pytest in any pace you like.

Check out http://pytest.org/latest/adopt.html if you are interested. :smile:

Cheers! :beers:

issue_title: on_partitions_revoked called with all partitions, not only the ones being revoked
issue_body:
When kafka is rebalancing the partitions, the `on_partitions_revoked` method will be called for all assigned partitions, not only the onces that are revoked. This was reproduced in kafka-python 1.3.2.

According to the documentation, a use case of this function is to be able to flush cached data for a partition that is no longer assigned. With the current behavior such function would flush the whole cache, even if there where only a new partition being assigned.

I looked at the code and came up with a possible solution. I'm new to this code, so there might be something that I have missed.

1. Store the currently assigned partitions in `ConsumerCoordinator._on_join_prepare`.
2. Move the call to `on_partitions_revoked` from `ConsumerCoordinator._on_join_prepare` to `ConsumerCoordinator._on_join_complete`.
3. Diff the previous and the new assigned partitions in `ConsumerCoordinator._on_join_complete` and call `on_partitions_revoked` and `on_partitions_assigned` with the correct partitions.

issue_title: Customizing the dispatcher for the Flask back end is difficult.
issue_body:
Since `Dispatcher` acts as a collection, it evaluates as `False` when empty. This causes the assignment `self.dispatcher = dispatcher or Dispatcher()` to make a new `Dispatcher` instance when passed an empty `Dispatcher`.

To reproduce:
```
class SubDispatcher(Dispatcher):
    pass

type(JSONRPCAPI(SubDispatcher()).dispatcher) == Dispatcher # This should be SubDispatcher
```

issue_title: Ansible inventory loading targets incorrect hosts
issue_body:
Molecule writes out an Ansible inventory file using the yaml format.  We then invoke the testinfra verifier and pass the yaml inventory file.

The testinfra tests are written and rely [testinfra_hosts](https://github.com/retr0h/molecule-vagrant-bug/blob/master/molecule/default/tests/test_default.py#L5-L6).  However, when doing this testinfra targets the wrong hosts when using 'all'.

This can be reproduced by:

* Checkout https://github.com/retr0h/molecule-vagrant-bug
* cd into the checkout
* `pip install -r bad-requirements.txt`
* run `molecule create` (on a system that has virtualbox, vagrant)
* run `molecule verify`

You will see the following:

```
============================= test session starts ==============================
    platform darwin -- Python 2.7.13, pytest-3.6.3, py-1.5.4, pluggy-0.6.0 -- /Users/jodewey/git/molecule-vagrant-bug/venv/bin/python2.7
    rootdir: /Users/jodewey/git/molecule-vagrant-bug/molecule/default, inifile:
    plugins: testinfra-1.13.1
collected 2 items

    tests/test_default.py::test_hosts_file[ansible://ubuntu-bionic64] ubuntu-xenial64
    PASSED
    tests/test_default.py::test_hosts_file[ansible://ubuntu-xenial64] ubuntu-bionic64
    PASSED

=========================== 2 passed in 1.93 seconds ===========================
```

As you see the Testinfra connection URL (which testinfra constructs) does not match the `hostname` print output from the test.

This problem started with Testinfra 1.13.1.  It appears to be fine in 1.13.0.

```
pip install -r working-requirements.txt
molecule verify

============================= test session starts ==============================
    platform darwin -- Python 2.7.13, pytest-3.6.3, py-1.5.4, pluggy-0.6.0 -- /Users/jodewey/git/molecule-vagrant-bug/venv/bin/python2.7
    rootdir: /Users/jodewey/git/molecule-vagrant-bug/molecule/default, inifile:
    plugins: testinfra-1.13.0
collected 2 items

    tests/test_default.py::test_hosts_file[ansible://ubuntu-xenial64] ubuntu-xenial64
    PASSED
    tests/test_default.py::test_hosts_file[ansible://ubuntu-bionic64] ubuntu-bionic64
    PASSED
=========================== 2 passed in 2.15 seconds ===========================
```

issue_title: tablesorter with pager - force update when table is empty
issue_body:
I am using `tablesorter` with the `pager` plugin. All of the paging is done server-side so I am using the `ajaxUrl`, `ajaxObject` and `customUrl` options of the pager to handle all the ajax calls needed whenever the user is flipping pages.

Inside the `customUrl` option I have a function that retrieves a timerange that the user defines in an external field, and add it into the url, in order to filter only entries within that range.

When the user changes that timerange I remove all of the rows from the table using `$("tr", tableElement).remove();` and then `$(tableElement).trigger("update");` to trigger the ajax call by the pager plugin.

This works when the table has entries in it: The entries get removed, then the update trigger issues a new ajax request, and gets the rows within the selected timerange inside the table with proper pagination.

**Problem:** When the currently selected range is empty (i.e. no entries were found), and the user selects a new range, the update trigger doesn't see any change in the data/entries (within the table's structure), thus the ajax request doesn't get issued.

*Is there a way to force the table to update itself and run the ajax query procedure?*

Question cross-listed here: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/28054625/tablesorter-with-pager-force-update-when-table-is-empty

issue_title: Output coroutine functions
issue_body:
It would be great if `@decorator` could be used to create decorators which produce [coroutine functions](https://docs.python.org/3/library/inspect.html#inspect.iscoroutinefunction).  Is there already such functionality and I just didn't find it? If not, would this be feasible? If so, are there any plans for this? Would you accept PRs?

Example:
```python
from inspect import iscoroutinefunction
from decorator import decorator

@decorator
async def ensure_coro(function, *args, **kwargs): 
    ret = function(*args, **kwargs)
    if iscoroutinefunction(function):
        ret = await ret
    return ret

@ensure_coro
def foo():
    return 42

assert iscoroutinefunction(foo)
```

issue_title: versorted should follow SemVer rules
issue_body:
## Minimum, Complete, Verifiable Example

```python
In [1]: import natsort

In [2]: a = ['1.0.0-alpha', '1.0.0-alpha.1', '1.0.0-alpha.beta', '1.0.0-beta', '1.0.0-beta.2', '1.0.0
   ...: -beta.11', '1.0.0-rc.1', '1.0.0']

In [3]: natsort.versorted(a)
Out[3]: 
['1.0.0',
 '1.0.0-alpha',
 '1.0.0-alpha.1',
 '1.0.0-alpha.beta',
 '1.0.0-beta',
 '1.0.0-beta.2',
 '1.0.0-beta.11',
 '1.0.0-rc.1']

In [4]: natsort.__version__
Out[4]: '5.3.2'

```

According to https://semver.org/, `1.0.0-alpha < 1.0.0-alpha.1 < 1.0.0-alpha.beta < 1.0.0-beta < 1.0.0-beta.2 < 1.0.0-beta.11 < 1.0.0-rc.1 < 1.0.0`.  The `1.0.0` is in the wrong place.

## Error message, Traceback, Desired behavior, Suggestion, Request, or Question

[There is a useful hack to make this work](http://natsort.readthedocs.io/en/master/examples.html#rc-sorting), but that should not be needed for a function called `versorted`. It should handle this out-of-the-box.

This would be a breaking change, and would require updating the `natsort` major version.

issue_title: Sorting ranges with ">" and "<"
issue_body:
Is there a way to sort the list:

["<1", "1-5", "6-10.5", ">10.5"]

I did a quick thing for my code like this:

```python
import re
import natsort

def bigsmall(line):
    def replace(match):
        val = float(match.group(2))
        return "{0}".format(val + 1 if match.group(1) == ">" else val - 1)
    return re.sub(r"([<>])=?(\d+\.?\d*)", replace, line)

ranges = ["<1", "1-5", "6-10.5", ">10.5"]
print(natsort.natsorted(ranges), "default sort")
print(natsort.natsorted(ranges, key=bigsmall), "with key")
```

issue_title: Change order of arguments in soundfile.write()?
issue_body:
We had some discussions in the past about argument ordering (#54, #78), but I don't think we really talked about the order of the filename and the array argument in `soundfile.write()`.

I always thought it would make sense to write the array first and the filename later.
I would ask "What?" first, before I want to know "Where?".
It also makes some kind of sense if you think about the array as an "input" argument and the filename as an "output" argument. I'd normally mention the input arguments before the output arguments.

However, while I'm using the function, I come to think more and more that the order actually seems strange.
What bugged me in the beginning, is that the array and its sample rate (which belong together), are actually separated in the call (and the sample rate is obligatory!):

```Python
sf.write(data, 'my_audio_file.wav', fs)
```

Then I looked around and found that it's actually quite common to mention the file name first, e.g.:
http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.savetxt.html
http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.save.html
http://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.io.wavfile.write.html#scipy.io.wavfile.write
http://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.io.savemat.html
http://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.io.mmwrite.html#scipy.io.mmwrite

I hereby suggest that we change the order to:

```Python
sf.write('my_audio_file.wav', data, fs)
```

Apart from it being the way NumPy and SciPy do it, it would also be more consistent to (and compatible to) `soundfile.read()`, where the array and the sample rate are returned right next to each other.

Are there any counter-examples that mention the data to be saved before the file name?
Are there any other arguments?

issue_title: Global_setter for services
issue_body:
I think it might be useful to implement something like a `global_setter` for services. Those should get called when one or more char values of this service are about to be changed to provide an  access point for end users.

The concrete case is an issue / bug with the HomeKit lightbulb service. When setting the light to a specific brightness in the Home app from the off state, HomeKit calls to char `client_update_values`, one for the state (on / off)  and one for the brightness char.
Reacting to both with the currently used `setter_callbacks` could lead to flickering if say the light is first set to on (100%) and only then to the wanted brightness.

Links:
 - https://github.com/home-assistant/home-assistant/issues/13768
 - https://github.com/home-assistant/homebridge-homeassistant/issues/218
 - https://github.com/nfarina/homebridge/issues/807

issue_title: Password & Username verification should use regex
issue_body:
So, I was randomly poking around in `Flask-User` today, curious about how passwords were verified & checked. I was really surprised when I found [the code](https://github.com/lingthio/Flask-User/blob/v0.6/flask_user/forms.py#L25). What is currently being done to validate passwords and usernames is very inefficient & unnecessary. Regex is a perfect tool for validating passwords, usernames, emails, and a number of other string- and pattern-based rules.

I believe `Flask-User` is in the midst of active `0.9` development, and thought I'd just drop in a quick note that this could be drastically improved with regex.

For example, password/username validation can be simplified to something like this and still match the same rules:

```python
from re import compile

PASSWORD_REGEX = compile(r'\A(?=\S*?\d)(?=\S*?[A-Z])(?=\S*?[a-z])\S{6,}\Z')
USERNAME_REGEX = compile(r'\A[\w\-\.]{3,}\Z')

def password_is_valid(password):
    return PASSWORD_REGEX.match(password) is not None

def username_is_valid(username):
    return USERNAME_REGEX.match(username) is not None
```

Just thought I'd bring it up for consideration. This really is the perfect use case for regex, and it's pretty odd to see passwords and usernames being validated by iterating over the entire string in Python code, counting how many times each character appears, and then checking those counts.

which lets users emulate an old 'default' install before by doing 'pip install Flask-User[defaults]', while not forcing those pieces to be installed and then uninstalled by others, who might prefer to have their project depend on 'Flask-User, PynamoDB, MyCustomSTMPEmailAdapter' or something.

issue_title: Requests or aiohttp?
issue_body:
Regenmaschine currently uses Requests for its HTTP library. While examining the Home Assistant platform (for which this library was originally created), I've decided that I want Regenmaschine to support asynchronous HTTP operations in the same way that Home Assistant does (via concurrency). Unfortunately, Requests doesn't support concurrency.

So, we have a decision: do we replace Requests with a concurrent HTTP library like aiohttp, or do we add on and support both?

# Pros for Replacement

* One library to manage instead of two
* Slightly smaller Regenmaschine package
* Fewer library exceptions to wrap
* Cleaner code overall

# Pros for Augmentation

* Supports more versions of Python
* Would make Regenmaschine applicable to an audience wider than the Home Assistant crowd

issue_title: Add a way to screen out importlib noise
issue_body:
I often use pyinstrument to profile importing a module to figure out ways to speed up imports. On python2 this works very nice but on python3 pyinstrument will pick up frames from importlib, making it much harder to visually see what's happening in the pyinstrument profile. This is happening because importlib was rewritten from C to python in python3, so pyinstrument is able to capture stack frames from it in python3.

To make that concrete, here's the profile for "import sympy" generated under python2:

https://gist.github.com/ngoldbaum/3e2f5213b45a64f67167d0dbd5878ad5

and python3:

https://gist.github.com/ngoldbaum/fb46b92f22a65181aa7442e2af2f51c1

        super().__init__()

        self.setWindowTitle("Chess GUI")
        self.setGeometry(300, 300, 800, 800)

        self.widgetSvg = QSvgWidget(parent=self)
        self.widgetSvg.setGeometry(10, 10, 600, 600)

        self.boardSize = min(self.widgetSvg.width(),
                             self.widgetSvg.height())
        self.coordinates = True
        self.margin = 0.05 * self.boardSize if self.coordinates else 0
        self.squareSize = (self.boardSize - 2 * self.margin) / 8.0

        self.board = chess.Board()
        self.pieceToMove = [None, None]

    @pyqtSlot(QWidget)
    def mousePressEvent(self, event):
        """
        Handle left mouse clicks and enable moving chess pieces by
        clicking on a chess piece and then the target square.

        Moves must be made according to the rules of chess because
        illegal moves are suppressed.
        """
        if event.x() <= self.boardSize and event.y() <= self.boardSize:
            if event.buttons() == Qt.LeftButton:
                if self.margin < event.x() < self.boardSize - self.margin and self.margin < event.y() < self.boardSize - self.margin:
                    file = int((event.x() - self.margin) / self.squareSize)
                    rank = 7 - int((event.y() - self.margin) / self.squareSize)
                    square = chess.square(file, rank)
                    piece = self.board.piece_at(square)
                    coordinates = "{}{}".format(chr(file + 97), str(rank + 1))
                    if self.pieceToMove[0] is not None:
                        move = chess.Move.from_uci("{}{}".format(self.pieceToMove[1], coordinates))
                        if move in self.board.legal_moves:
                            self.board.push(move)
                            if self.board.is_check():
                                print("Check!")
                        piece = None
                        coordinates = None
                    self.pieceToMove = [piece, coordinates]
                else:
                    # Envoke the paint event.
                    self.update()
        else:
            QWidget.mousePressEvent(self, event)

    @pyqtSlot(QWidget)
    def paintEvent(self, event):
        """
        Draw a chessboard with the starting position and then redraw
        it for every new move.
        """
        self.boardSvg = chess.svg.board(board=self.board,
                                        size=self.boardSize).encode("UTF-8")
        self.widgetSvg.load(self.boardSvg)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    chessTitan = QApplication(sys.argv)
    window = MainWindow()
    window.show()
    sys.exit(chessTitan.exec_())
```

This code creates an SVG chessboard in an application window. It allows the pieces to be moved according to the rules of chess. Use the left mouse button to click on a piece and again the left mouse button to click on the target square. If the move if valid, the piece will move to that square.

Currently, if there is check, I print out "Check!". How can I instead of printing "Check!" make that gradient color on the square that the checked King is on (either the White or the Black King)?

I know that the `chess.svg.board()` function has this `check` argument which marks a given square, say `chess.E8`, as being in check (only logical if a King is on that square and is really in check). But now I wonder whether this `check` argument can be somehow applied later? Is there a `mark_as_check` or something like that to be used after the fact for a given square? I've looked and haven't found anything. So, how can I implement this?

issue_title: Default "fuzzy" parameter value for Geonames
issue_body:
In Geocoder's wrapper for Geonames "fuzzy" is 0.8 by default. 
https://github.com/DenisCarriere/geocoder/blob/16a697240974b2cb66df56b534be43ef26218110/geocoder/geonames.py#L93

Is there some reasoning behind this value?

The Geonames' docs say:
http://www.geonames.org/export/geonames-search.html

>default is '1', defines the fuzziness of the search terms. float between 0 and 1. The search term is only applied to the name attribute.

Perhaps it is better to use the defaults suggested by the service documentation?

* * *

I probably wouldn't raise this issue if Geonames' idea of fuzziness wasn't about messing up search result relevance. For example, if I search for "Russia" with Geocoder's current defaults:

```
g = geocoder.geonames('Russia', key='KEY')
g.geojson
```

I will get a region in Jamaica called "Russia". However, as a user I would prefer to see countries first and regions second, but "fuzzy" interferes with result relevance. This behavior is easy to replicate without Geocoder by manually altering search query in the URL:

http://www.geonames.org/search.html?q=russia&country=&fuzzy=0.8

This is definitely not the package maintainers' fault, but since Geonames service doesn't do fuzziness right, perhaps it's a good idea to set fuzzy to 1.0, as their docs suggest?

issue_title: Add support for findings intervals that contain a given interval
issue_body:
Currently, `tree.search()` only supports findings intervals that overlap or are contained by a given interval. However, there is no support for finding intervals that contain a given interval.

This could be added in a straightforward manner by replacing the boolean `strict` option with one that accepts three values (overlap, contain, or contained by).

The code at the end of `search()` could be edited to something like:
```python
# option is a parameter to search() that specifies the search type
if option == 'contains':
    result = set(
        iv for iv in result
        if iv.begin <= begin and iv.end >= end
    )
```

issue_title: Include copy of license in source / tarballs
issue_body:
mimerender claims MIT as its license, but AFAICT does not include the text of the license anywhere in the source tree on github, nor in the release tarballs. The MIT license includes this text:

" The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all copies or substantial portions of the Software."

i.e. the license itself requires that it be included with distributions of the code, yet the canonical distribution of the code does not include it. As the Fedora licensing guidelines - https://fedoraproject.org/wiki/Packaging:LicensingGuidelines - and similar guidelines for other distributions (e.g. Debian) state, "In cases where the upstream has chosen a license that requires that a copy of the license text be distributed along with the binaries and/or source code, but does not provide a copy of the license text (in the source tree, or in some rare cases, anywhere), the packager should do their best to point out this confusion to upstream.", so I am doing that. Please include a copy of the license in the source. The usual way to do this would be to include the text in the top-level directory as a file named `LICENSE` or `COPYING`.

issue_title: reduceByKey() and groupByKey() throw errors with numpy arrays
issue_body:
Because `groupByKey()` uses `sorted()` with no key function, whenever keys are repeated (the only reason you'd want to reduce or group by keys anyway) and the comparators of numpy arrays are not well-defined, when an RDD has numpy arrays as *values*, a `ValueError` is thrown:

```python
import numpy as np
from operator import add
from pysparkling import Context as SparkContext

sc = SparkContext()

keys = (0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2)
values = (np.ones((2, 3)) * (i + k) for i, k in enumerate(keys))

rdd = sc.parallelize(zip(keys, values))
a = rdd.reduceByKey(add).collect()

print(a)
```

Result (Anaconda Python 3.5 on Linux):
```
$ python3.5 np_reduce_by_key.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "np_reduce_by_key.py", line 13, in <module>
    a = rdd.reduceByKey(add).collect()
  File "~/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pysparkling/rdd.py", line 1144, in reduceByKey
    return self.groupByKey().mapValues(lambda x: functools.reduce(f, x))
  File "~/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pysparkling/rdd.py", line 695, in groupByKey
    sorted(self.collect()),
ValueError: The truth value of an array with more than one element is ambiguous. Use a.any() or a.all()
```

On a pySpark instance, the result is (adjusted spacing):
```
[(0, array([[ 1.,  1.,  1.], [ 1.,  1.,  1.]])),
 (2, array([[13., 13., 13.], [13., 13., 13.]])),
 (1, array([[ 7.,  7.,  7.], [ 7.,  7.,  7.]]))]
```

Ideally, this operation wouldn't involve sorting at all, but I understand why it's done that way. I think the proper mitigation is to use:

``` python
from operator import itemgetter
def groupby(*args, **kwargs):
    ...
    sorted(self.collect(), key=itemgetter(1)),
    ...
```

I've been playing with asynqp a little bit, and I'm really liking it.

I'm trying to figure out how to handle reconnecting in the case of a lost connection. With a producer I get the following output on the console, but I don't seem to be able to capture it in the loop error handler.
   
    socket.send() raised exception

issue_title: Object that gets updated in safeSrc array is not updated in displayed array
issue_body:
I know it would be inefficient to scan *every single object* in the safeSrc array for changes each digest cycle in order to detect an updated object, so I found a reasonable work around. Update the value (safeSrc[index] = value), push a dummy value to the end, schedule a $timeout callback to remove from the end.

So I'd say this isn't a high priority to fix given that there is a workaround, but it would be nice to have a more elegant way of notifying the table that a value had been updated so that a hack like this isn't necessary. If you have any suggestions I might find time to submit a pull request with the changes.

issue_title: Notify on pipe completed
issue_body:
I have a requirement where one of my filtering directives needs to know when the result of getFilteredCollection method on stTableController changes, this so I'm able to hide some rows in an advanced dropdown multi select filter when no entries with the value exists in the filtered collection (customer requirements). 

When looking at the code I see there is no way of knowing when the property content changes or for that sake know when the pipe method is done without watching the results, which would downgrade performance significantly. Knowing when the pipe method is done would be valuable, I have several times used quirky workarounds to cope with the lack of any such "notification".

I have created 2 different implementations offering notification when pipe function is done, one where consumer may register a callback, and one where an event is broadcasted. I prefer the broadcasting, but included both so you may evaluate which direction we want to go.

Let me know what you think, and I'll put up a pull request with linted and commented code if you like one of the solutions.

issue_title: Django Social Auth Truck Factor
issue_body:
Django Social Auth Truck Factor

As part of my PhD research on code authorship, we calculated the Truck Factor (TF) of some popular GitHub repositories.

As you probably know, the [Truck (or Bus) Factor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bus_factor) designates the minimal number of developers that have to be hit by a truck (or quit) before a project is incapacitated. In our work, we consider that a system is in trouble if more than 50% of its files become orphan (i.e., without a main author).

More details on our work in this preprint: https://peerj.com/preprints/1233

We calculated the TF for **Django Social Auth** and obtained a value of **2**.

The developers responsible for this TF are:

Matias Aguirre - author of 91% of the files 
Stas Kravets- author of 15% of the files

To validate our results, we would like to ask **Django Social Auth** developers the following three brief questions:

(a) Do you agree that the listed developers are the main developers of **Django Social Auth**?

(b) Do you agree that **Django Social Auth** will be in trouble if the listed developers leave the project (e.g., if they win in the lottery, to be less morbid)?

(c) Does **Django Social Auth** have some characteristics that would attenuate the loss of the listed developers (e.g., detailed documentation)?

Thanks in advance for your collaboration,

Guilherme Avelino
PhD Student
Applied Software Engineering Group (ASERG)
UFMG, Brazil
http://aserg.labsoft.dcc.ufmg.br/

issue_title: Support extracting UTC ISOTime from 'GPS' and 'TT' timescales.
issue_body:
The [VOEvent spec](http://www.ivoa.net/documents/VOEvent/) specifies that timestamps (``TimeInstant``) for event details in the WhereWhen section can use the follow timescale standards: 

> UTC (Coordinated Universal Time [26]), TT (Terrestrial Time, currently 65.184
> seconds ahead of UTC), GPS time, or TDB (Barycentric Dynamical Time). 

If I understand correctly, TT and GPS timestamps should be specified using the ``TimeOffset`` sub-element of the ``TimeInstant``, because that's how those standards work - but this isn't actually made explicit in the VOEvent standard, and there's no example other than UTC time. As a result I'm putting off implementing this until there's actually a use-case for it. If you have one, get in touch and I'll be happy help with it.

issue_title: The trimmed mean estimate is bad
issue_body:
The implementation of the trimmed mean estimate (trimmed_mean method) doesn't look right. The estimate seems way off from the real value. Here is an example:

``` python
import numpy as np
from tdigest import TDigest
```

Creat 10000 samples of random uniform distributition.

``` python
x = np.random.random(size=10000)*100
```

Create a T-Digest for this

``` python
d = TDigest()
d.batch_update(x)
```

Estimate the trimmed mean of X that above the 25% percentile.

``` python
tm_estimate = d.trimmed_mean(25,100)
print(tm_estimate)
```

```
75.0410094085
```

Now, find the real 25% percentile and compujte the real trimmed mean.

``` python
x_25 = np.percentile(x,25)

x_trimmed = x[x>=x_25]
tm_real = x_trimmed.mean()
print(tm_real)
```

```
62.3013933259
```

issue_title: Add option for logging all requests
issue_body:
As a first pass:

- Log during `app.after_request` function
- Use boolean config value `LOGGING_REQUESTS` for enabling/disabling
- Use string config value `LOGGING_REQUESTS_LOGGER` to set which logger to use
- Optionally use `app.logger` as main logger (maybe?) if `LOGGING_REQUESTS_LOGGER` not set.
- Either use `LOGGING_REQUESTS_FORMAT` for the log record's message or require that the logger configure itself to properly log these requests (i.e. provide a preset of request/response values to the logging call). Can also use `LOGGING_REQUESTS_FORMAT` to build an `extra` log record value that would then get piped to the log formatters.

Unique validators bis
issue_body:
This is related to issue #27 . I did exactly as described there:

```python
obj = session.query(Login).get(primary_key)
form = LoginForm(obj=obj)
form.populate_obj(obj)
form.validate()
```

where 

```python
class LoginForm(ModelForm):
    class Meta:
        model = Login
        only = ['username', 'password', 'confirm']
        info = {'password': {'form_field_class': PasswordField}},
```

and 

```python
class Login(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'login'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    username = Column(Unicode(32), nullable=False, unique=True, info={'label': _('Benutzername')})
    password = Column(String(255), nullable=False, info={'label': _('Passwort'), 'field_type': PasswordField, 'form_field_class': PasswordField, 'validators': [EqualTo('confirm', message=_('Passwörter müssen übereinstimmen'))]})  # hashed password
    confirm = Column(String(255), nullable=False, info={'label': _('Passwort wiederholen'), 'field_type': PasswordField, 'form_field_class': PasswordField})  # Confirm

    salt = Column(String(255), nullable=True)  # salt for hashing password
```

The `form.validate()` returns `False` for every single item:

```python
{'password': ['This field is required.'], 'username': ['This field is required.'], 'confirm': ['This field is required.']}
```
This is because the validator `InputRequired` is added to each field (which checks each field's raw_data which is not set by above)

As a test, I changed my code to the following:

```python
obj = session.query(Login).get(primary_key)
form = LoginForm(obj=obj)
for name, field in form._fields.items():
            field.raw_data = field.data
form.populate_obj(obj)
form.validate()
```

Giving me:

```python
{'username': ['Already exists.']}
```
Which is better, but the Unique constraint seamingly misses the fact that we are editing an already existing item.

Is there anything else where I can make this work again?

My settings

* Python 3.4
and 
* SQLAlchemy (1.0.8)
* SQLAlchemy-Utils (0.30.17)
* validators (0.8)
* WTForms (2.0.2)
* WTForms-Alchemy (0.13.3)
* WTForms-Components (0.9.7)

issue_title: documentation
Problem with saving file on s3 storage. How save works?
issue_body:
Since version 1.1.9+ (ofter fork) you changed S3BotoStorageFile _file attribute from StringIO to SpooledTemporaryFile.

In my code I use PIL for generate picture thumbnail like this:
```python
img = Image.open(default_storage.open(instance.uploaded.name, 'r'))
img.thumbnail(meta_data['size'], Image.ANTIALIAS)
f = default_storage.open(str('%s' + meta_data['prefix'] + '%s') % (file_path, filename,), 'w')
img.save(f, ext, quality=90)
f.close()
```
and weird think was that it works for png but not for jpg files.
After some investigation (https://github.com/python-pillow/Pillow/issues/1940) I found that there is two ways of saving files in Pillow
```python
# file ImageFile.py
    try:
        fh = fp.fileno()
        fp.flush()
    except (AttributeError, io.UnsupportedOperation):
        # compress to Python file-compatible object
        for e, b, o, a in tile:
            e = Image._getencoder(im.mode, e, a, im.encoderconfig)
            if o > 0:
                fp.seek(o, 0)
            e.setimage(im.im, b)
            while True:
                l, s, d = e.encode(bufsize)
                fp.write(d)
                if s:
                    break
            if s < 0:
                raise IOError("encoder error %d when writing image file" % s)
            e.cleanup()
    else:
        # slight speedup: compress to real file object
        for e, b, o, a in tile:
            e = Image._getencoder(im.mode, e, a, im.encoderconfig)
            if o > 0:
                fp.seek(o, 0)
            e.setimage(im.im, b)
            s = e.encode_to_file(fh, bufsize)
            if s < 0:
                raise IOError("encoder error %d when writing image file" % s)
            e.cleanup()
```
First way (when error is throw) call python's file write method and second no.

It works for version 1.1.8 where was use StringIO and doesn't work for newer versions where SpooledTemporaryFile is used.

Is this bug? How to fix it?

issue_title: altering user_loader
Change to is_authenticated requires applications to enforce flask-login version to prevent security issues
issue_body:
I know that flask-login is not 1.0 yet and thus is allowed to make breaking changes. This is not about the fact of change itself, but a peculiar side-effect of the change of which I am not sure you were aware when making the change.

I would like to point out that the change of is_authenticated from method to property leads to code which **silently** treats unauthenticated users as authenticated if run with old flask versions.

The reason being that ``bool(foo.is_authenticated)`` is always true if ``is_authenticated`` is a method, which it is in older versions of flask-login. This should probably be mentioned more explicitly in the documentation and possibly the changelog, to prevent application developers to run into the open knife, especially those who start using flask-login only now and are not aware of the vastly different behaviour in old versions.

Thank you for your consideration.

issue_title: artifact upload: add option --classifier
Performing multiple queries without initialisation overhead.
issue_body:
Hi. I am using reverse_geocoder inside an Apache Spark job on a couple of million entries. 

Due to the nature of map reduce jobs, it will be a lot easier to call rg.search() on a single coordinate a million times, rather than calling it once with a list of million coordinates. 

However, due to the nature of how the Geocoder is initialised, the overhead from re-initialising makes it much slower (infeasibly so).

While there are tricks to get around that, it is creating a lot of headaches, so my question is, it it possible to initialise and instance of the geocoder once and have it run N times on one entry with roughly the same performance it would if ran one time on N entries. 

If not, are there any plans for including that? Thanks!

issue_title: Suggestion - show how to add a user to a list towards the top of the README
issue_body:
I believe the following is a rather common use case:

- sign up for MailChimp
- create a list
- subscribe users to this list when they sign up

It takes a few minutes to figure out the solution:

```python
client = Mailchimp(...)
client.member.create('<list_id>', {
    'email_address': 'john.doe@example.com',
    'status': 'subscribed',
    'merge_fields': {
        'FNAME': 'John',
        'LNAME': 'Doe',
    },
})
```

The onboarding experience would be smoother if the README showed that example (or a similar one).

I hope this feedback helps!

issue_title: Is it safe to use SECRET_KEY as the salt?
issue_body:
Looks like it's possible to discover the salt by looking at hashids: http://carnage.github.io/2015/08/cryptanalysis-of-hashids
This blog post is linked at hashids.org website: http://hashids.org/#decoding

Therefore, maybe this lib shouldn't use `SECRET_KEY` by default as the salt.
IMO there should be a custom Django setting for the global hashid salt.

" % text.replace('\n', '
'))) text = text.replace('',r'\textbf{').replace('','}') #some replacement of HTML formatting text = text.replace('',r'\textit{').replace('','}') text = text.replace('',r'\texttt{').replace('','}') display(JSON({'caption': text, 'label':label}),include=["application/json"]) ``` - In LenvsLatexPreprocessor.preprocess_cell function populate the output cells metadata with a figure with adjacent JSON field: ```python if cell.cell_type == "code" and "outputs" in cell: json_metadata = [] mime_types = ['image/svg+xml','image/png','image/jpeg','application/pdf'] for output in cell.outputs: if "data" in output: if 'application/json' in output.data and 'caption' in output.data['application/json']: #found a json field with caption json_metadata.append(output.data['application/json']) if any(x in output.data for x in mime_types) and len(json_metadata)>0: output.metadata.update(json_metadata.pop()) # write caption to data field metadata ``` - Use the template structure described in [Making publication ready Python Notebooks](http://blog.juliusschulz.de/blog/ultimate-ipython-notebook#figures-1) Replace cell.metadata.caption with output.metadata.caption and cell.metadata.label with output.metadata.label in this template Hope you find this way to generate figure captions interesting...

issue_title: chatserver example templates in Django 1.8+

From Django 1.8 and onwards the TEMPLATE_* settings are depricated in settings.py, and will raise a `TemplateDoesNotExist` exception on those versions of Django. The following can be replaced:

```
TEMPLATE_DEBUG = ...

TEMPLATE_LOADERS = ...
```
with the following
```
TEMPLATES = [
    {
        'BACKEND': 'django.template.backends.django.DjangoTemplates',
        'DIRS': [],
        'APP_DIRS': True,
        'OPTIONS': {
            'context_processors': [

issue_title: Entries not filtered correctly on Entry.published.on_site
issue_body:
[These lines](https://github.com/Fantomas42/django-blog-zinnia/blob/develop/zinnia/managers.py#L54-L55) call the super-implementation of `get_queryset` and therefore do **not** call the method on the `EntryPublishedManager` class itself – thus skipping the check on `status` and start/end of publication, leading to the availability of unpublished entries when calling `Entry.published.on_site()`.

In the tests [this assert](https://github.com/Fantomas42/django-blog-zinnia/blob/develop/zinnia/tests/test_managers.py#L119) should return `1` as `entry_2` in the test is not published! There are some other test cases that begin to fail when fixing this bug.

As I discovered while tryin to fix it, this bug has some bigger consequences:

- the `on_site` call is used [in `EntryDetail`](https://github.com/Fantomas42/django-blog-zinnia/blob/develop/zinnia/views/entries.py#L26).
- a correct checking of publication status in `on_site` would mean that non-published objects do not appear in the queryset anymore
- this breaks the [EntryPreviewMixin](https://github.com/Fantomas42/django-blog-zinnia/blob/develop/zinnia/views/mixins/entry_preview.py) which expects to check the resulting object individually (however, no unpublished object will ever get there).

This needs some design decisions, so I opened an issue rather than a PR.

======================================================================
ERROR: test_add_tag (taggit.tests.tests.TaggableManagerCustomPKTestCase)

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/ivan/Sites/django-taggit/taggit/tests/tests.py", line 135, in test_add_tag
    apple.tags.remove('green')
  File "/home/ivan/Sites/django-taggit/taggit/utils.py", line 125, in inner
    return func(self, *args, **kwargs)

    query = self.query.clone()
  File "/home/ivan/Sites/django-taggit/bootstrap/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/db/models/sql/query.py", line 259, in clone
    obj.where = copy.deepcopy(self.where, memo=memo)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/copy.py", line 174, in deepcopy
    y = copier(memo)
  File "/home/ivan/Sites/django-taggit/bootstrap/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/utils/tree.py", line 61, in __deepcopy__
    obj.children = copy.deepcopy(self.children, memodict)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/copy.py", line 163, in deepcopy

    obj.children = copy.deepcopy(self.children, memodict)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/copy.py", line 163, in deepcopy
    y = copier(x, memo)

issue_title: Automatic indexing when object created
issue_body:
Hi,

Great work on this project.

I am having trouble with automatically indexing newly created objects with `groupby`.

Every time a new model is created, it should index it.

However, it only indexes the "title" value. It does not index the "tag".

So, I created a manual function `manual_index`. When I run that, the "tag" value does get added to the index. However this process has to be manually triggered.

How can I get the "tag" value to save every time a new object is created?

***Search.py***

```
class TaskIndex(DocType):
    title = String()
    class Meta:
        index = 'task-index'

def manual_index():
    TaskIndex.init()
    es = Elasticsearch()
    bulk(client=es, actions=(b.indexing() for b in models.Task.objects.all().iterator()))
```


**Models.py**


```
from itertools import groupby

class Tag(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField("Name", max_length=5000, blank=True)
    taglevel = models.IntegerField("Tag level", null=True, blank=True)

class Item(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField("Title", max_length=10000, blank=True)
    tag = models.ManyToManyField('Tag', blank=True)

    def get_grouped_tags(self):
        tag = self.tag.order_by('taglevel')
        grouped_tags = {
            tag_level: [
                { 'name': tag_of_level.name, 'taglevel': tag_of_level.taglevel, }
                for tag_of_level in tags_of_level
            ] for tag_level, tags_of_level
            in groupby(tag, lambda tag: tag.taglevel)
        }
        return grouped_tags

    def indexing(self):
        obj = TaskIndex(
            meta={'id': self.id},
            title=self.title,
            tag=self.get_grouped_tags()
        obj.save()
        return obj.to_dict(include_meta=True)
```

issue_title: redefine #CHECK in sframe plugin
warp_size
issue_body:
For the GPU kernels, it'd be good to have a defined constant for the warp size so that it doesn't have to be hard coded in each file (see for example `static const unsigned WARP_SIZE = 32` in `batch_norm.cu`). 
Reading the `warpSize` from the `cudaDeviceProp` wouldn't be a good solution in my opinion in terms of performance.
If NVIDIA decides to change the warp size on a future architecture, it could then be easily be switched depending on `__CUDA_ARCH__`.

issue_title: Bug in mxnet.contrib.text.utils.count_tokens_from_str

mxnet.contrib.text.utils.count_tokens_from_str(source_str, token_delim=' ', seq_delim='\n', to_lower=False, counter_to_update=None)[source]

the description is
Counts tokens in the specified string.

For token_delim=’’ and seq_delim=’’, a specified string of two sequences of tokens may look like:
```
<td>token1<td>token2<td>token3<td><sd><td>token4<td>token5<td><sd>

I'm trying to call it like so:

f=open("file.txt","r")

Any help you an offer is appreciated.

issue_title: South Migrations with PostgreSQLUUIDField Create CharField Incorrectly
issue_body: The PostgreSQLUUIDField does not define it's own south_field_tripple() method and falls back to the one defined by UUIDField. Because of this, south migrations create these fields using a CharField type instead of a native UUID field type.

issue_title: About the quality of this repository
issue_body:
Hi dear maintainers,

I've been pretty active on this repository for a long time now, and I must say that I'm pretty pissed with the quality of the code. I know that _THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND_ but, still, you cannot just put your official python SDK out there and say "oh it's fine if its broken, just use PHP instead".

Where I'm working, we've been using the Facebook Ads API for several years and went through all the different migration adventures, not without breaking a sweat. I must say that when I saw the [changelog](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/apps/changelog) for the 2.6 version, I told myself "Piece of cake". Yeah, right. I've opened 4 issues on this repo so far:
- https://github.com/facebook/facebook-python-ads-sdk/pull/222
- https://github.com/facebook/facebook-python-ads-sdk/pull/223
- https://github.com/facebook/facebook-python-ads-sdk/issues/224
- https://github.com/facebook/facebook-python-ads-sdk/issues/221

And the deadline to migrate on this version is set 5 days from now, July 13th.

But that's not all. You introduced an auto-generation of code. I don't know who had this idea but it's pretty far from genius. So I get it, you don't want to have to maintain 250+ python classes manually. But hey, have you thought about inheritance? Have you thought about meta classes? Instead here is what we get:
- obfuscated code: I didn't know it could exist in Python, but you made it possible
- almost impossible collaboration: a nice guy wants to improve your data model? he can't
- poorly designed python code: yes, python is supposed to be a scripting language. But do you think the Django guys are writing scripts?
- non-standard code: have you tried to run `pep8` or `pylint` on your codebase?

Oh and that's still not all. There is simply no unit tests. The only ones that are here were coded by collaborators not working at Facebook. Today, you have:
- 32 unit tests
- 7 integration tests (BTW, do you even run them?)
- 250+ python files

Don't you think that instead of auto-generating python code you could have written unit tests?

Last but not least, today you have 30 opened issues/PRs, and some have been opened for a long time. Can you improve your processes regarding that matter? I think it's sad that people are spending time on this SDK and you just ignore their work.

Of course I'm gonna continue to use this repo because I have no other choice, but I and other developers would be very glad if you could work on those improvements.

Have a good day,
Max.

issue_title: get_roles inconsistent
issue_body:
For users: `get_roles('foo', obj=bar, inherit=False)` uses `obj.get_local_roles_for_userid(username)` which ignores LocalRoles-Adapters.

For groups it uses `lrmanager.getRolesInContext(plone_user, obj)` which includes local_roles set on parents.

Both are wrong should instead return roles that are set only on this object including those provided by LocalRoles-Adapters or from groups that the user is a member of but ignore global roles and roles from parents.

I'm working on a pull-request.

issue_title: [Support] Create Patch for new file from buffer
issue_body:
Hello all,

I am working with the use case of creating diffs from buffers. Everything is going well as there is decent support for it, especially in the form of `Blob.diff_to_buffer`. The function works great when changing content and will soon work for deleting a file (In #741). However, I cannot figure out a proper way to add files using `Blob.diff_to_buffer`.

Do you guys have any suggestions? In libgit2, the proper way is to call `git_diff_blob_to_buffer` and set the `Blob*` argument to `NULL`. However, pygit2 does not have a static function to be able to do this. Do you guys have any suggestions? Here are things I can think of:

1. Create a special "Empty" blob object (or give it an `exists` property) that can be used to signify a nonexistent blob and then do `diff_to_buffer` on that.

2. Expose a static `Patch.from_blob_to_buffer()` which can then be called like `Patch.from_blob_to_buffer(None, buf)`.

The other thing I can think of, which does not yet exist, is adding a `Tree.diff_to_buffer()`/`Tree.diff_to_blob()` to libgit2, which would solve this problem since the Tree would know whether the blob is being added or removed. (Of course, this would then have to be wrapped in pygit2)

Thoughts?

issue_title: support using the transaction inside exception views
issue_body:
A major short-coming in the current implementation is that the transaction has already been committed or aborted by the time an exception view is executed since the tween lies UNDER the EXCVIEW.

This can cause weird errors in your app because you may have transactional items attached to the request and thus the references are still around in the exception view. Also it'd often times nice to be able to continue doing queries, etc even if the changes will later be aborted.

The most common scenario can be solved by having `zope.sqlalchemy` avoid expiring objects on flush, but this doesn't solve the issue of performing more queries in the exception views. It just allows already-loaded objects to be used.

My proposal is to split pyramid_tm into 2 tweens that wrap the EXCVIEW. The OVER tween will perform the commit/abort while the UNDER tween will perform retries on normal views and mark state for later abort if exceptions are found. The transaction is kept alive until after the exception view has executed.
